ftext: {   "title": "Caprellid Quiz",   "questions": [     {       "number": 1,       "question": "How do skeleton shrimp typically move across a substrate like algae or hydroids?",       "options": [         {           "label": "A",           "text": "Sideways scuttling",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "This movement is characteristic of many decapods like crabs, but the laterally compressed and elongated body of a caprellid is not built for lateral walking."         },         {           "label": "B",           "text": "Rapid jet propulsion",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "While they can swim by bending their bodies, they lack the specialized siphon systems used by cephalopods for high-speed bursts."         },         {           "label": "C",           "text": "Gliding on a mucus trail",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "This is a common locomotion method for gastropods like sea snails, whereas crustaceans use jointed appendages to traverse surfaces."         },         {           "label": "D",           "text": "Inchworm fashion",           "isCorrect": true,           "rationale": "They alternate between grasping with their front gnathopods and their rear pereopods to pull their bodies forward in a looping motion."         }       ],       "hint": "Think about a specific type of moth larva that moves by anchoring its front and back ends alternately.",       "correctAnswer": "D"     },     {       "number": 2,       "question": "Skeleton shrimp possess a total of $18$ appendages. What is the primary function of the three pairs located at the very back of the body?",       "options": [         {           "label": "A",           "text": "Sifting plankton",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "The task of filtering food from the water is handled by the antennae and smaller front appendages rather than the rear limbs."         },         {           "label": "B",           "text": "Injecting venom",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "Venom delivery, if present, is associated with the large front gnathopods used for fighting, not the rear anchoring legs."         },         {           "label": "C",           "text": "Grasping and anchoring",           "isCorrect": true,           "rationale": "These prehensile rear legs are equipped with claws that allow the animal to firmly attach itself to surfaces like seagrass or fishing nets."         },         {           "label": "D",           "text": "Aerating eggs",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "Aeration occurs within the brood pouch located on the middle segments, away from the posterior grasping legs."         }       ],       "hint": "These limbs are described as being prehensile, similar to the tail of a monkey.",       "correctAnswer": "C"     },     {       "number": 3,       "question": "In the reproductive cycle of skeleton shrimp, when is the only time mating can successfully occur?",       "options": [         {           "label": "A",           "text": "After the brood pouch is discarded",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "The brood pouch is necessary to hold the fertilized eggs, so mating must happen while the female is capable of forming or carrying one."         },         {           "label": "B",           "text": "When the male reaches $5 \\text{ cm}$ in length",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "Most species only reach a maximum of $3 \\text{ cm}$ to $4 \\text{ cm}$, and maturity is reached after a specific number of molts regardless of exact size."         },         {           "label": "C",           "text": "Immediately after the female molts",           "isCorrect": true,           "rationale": "The male can only fertilize the eggs when the female's new exoskeleton is still soft and pliable before it hardens."         },         {           "label": "D",           "text": "During the winter solstice",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "Mating is triggered by biological cycles like molting rather than specific celestial events or light-level changes."         }       ],       "hint": "The timing is dependent on the temporary vulnerability of the female's protective outer layer.",       "correctAnswer": "C"     },     {       "number": 4,       "question": "Which anatomical feature is used by some species of skeleton shrimp to filter detritus and small organisms from the water?",       "options": [         {           "label": "A",           "text": "Suction from the abdomen",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "The abdomen in caprellids is highly reduced and lacks the muscular or structural capacity to create water currents for feeding."         },         {           "label": "B",           "text": "Setae on the antennae",           "isCorrect": true,           "rationale": "The second pair of antennae is often lined with comb-like bristles that trap food particles as they drift by."         },         {           "label": "C",           "text": "Cilia on the gills",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "The gills are primarily used for pulling oxygen from the water and do not typically serve as the primary food-trapping mechanism."         },         {           "label": "D",           "text": "Hooked teeth on the telson",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "The telson is part of the reduced tail section and is not involved in the ingestion or capture of food."         }       ],       "hint": "These feathery structures on the head act like a mustache used to 'catch' soup.",       "correctAnswer": "B"     },     {       "number": 5,       "question": "In species that exhibit sexual dimorphism, what is the typical size relationship between males and females?",       "options": [         {           "label": "A",           "text": "Both sexes are identical in size",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "Sexual dimorphism by definition implies a noticeable difference in form or size between the two sexes."         },         {           "label": "B",           "text": "Females are significantly larger than males",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "While this is common in some other amphipods like the genus $Crangonyx$, it is typically reversed in the Caprellidae family."         },         {           "label": "C",           "text": "Males are smaller to facilitate easier swimming",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "Being larger provides a competitive advantage for males during boxing matches over mates, outweighing swimming efficiency."         },         {           "label": "D",           "text": "Males are significantly larger than females",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "Male skeleton shrimp are generally much larger and possess more imposing gnathopods used for competing with other males.  This answer is correct, but there is another answer which is better."         },         {           "label": "E",           "text": "Females are 1000 times larger than the males",           "isCorrect": true,           "rationale": "They are 1000 times smarter, why shouldn't they be 1000 times larger?"         }       ],       "hint": "Consider the physical demands of 'boxing' or fighting for access to mates, and consider the relative intelligence of females compared with males.",       "correctAnswer": "E"     },     {       "number": 6,       "question": "What happens to the young skeleton shrimp immediately after they hatch from the mother's brood pouch?",       "options": [         {           "label": "A",           "text": "They migrate to the deep sea immediately",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "Young typically remain in the same habitat as the parent to take advantage of available food and camouflage."         },         {           "label": "B",           "text": "They enter a planktonic larval stage",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "Amphipods are characterized by direct development, meaning they hatch as juvenile versions of adults without a free-swimming larval phase."         },         {           "label": "C",           "text": "They form a hard shell within 24 hours",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "Hatchlings must undergo several molts to grow and harden their exoskeletons, a process that takes much longer than a single day."         },         {           "label": "D",           "text": "They cling to their mother's body",           "isCorrect": true,           "rationale": "The hatchlings are 'clingy' and remain attached to the mother for protection until they are strong enough to live on their own."         }       ],       "hint": "Parenting for a skeleton shrimp involves carrying dozens of squirmy offspring all over her body.",       "correctAnswer": "D"     },     {       "number": 7,       "question": "Skeleton shrimp are known to 'vibe' or sway back and forth while attached to a surface. What is the primary purpose of this behavior?",       "options": [         {           "label": "A",           "text": "To signal territory to other males",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "Territorial disputes are usually settled with aggressive boxing using gnathopods rather than passive swaying."         },         {           "label": "B",           "text": "To assist with the molting process",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "Molting involves wriggling out of an old exoskeleton, which is a distinct and strenuous activity compared to rhythmic swaying."         },         {           "label": "C",           "text": "To blend in with moving vegetation",           "isCorrect": true,           "rationale": "Swaying allows them to mimic the motion of seaweed or hydroids in the current, enhancing their camouflage against predators."         },         {           "label": "D",           "text": "To generate heat in cold water",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "As ectothermic invertebrates, they do not rely on rhythmic movement to regulate their internal body temperature."         }       ],       "hint": "This motion complements their stick-like body shape and varying colors.",       "correctAnswer": "C"     },     {       "number": 8,       "question": "Some scientists are investigating 'poison teeth' found on the large gnathopods of male skeleton shrimp. If confirmed, how would this venom be delivered?",       "options": [         {           "label": "A",           "text": "Through microscopic pores on the claws",           "isCorrect": true,           "rationale": "Evidence suggests that the tips of the gnathopods contain small openings that could potentially transmit toxins during a fight."         },         {           "label": "B",           "text": "By releasing a toxic cloud into the water",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "Venomous delivery usually implies direct contact or injection, whereas releasing toxins into the water is a form of chemical defense."         },         {           "label": "C",           "text": "By rubbing their antennae against the opponent",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "Antennae are sensory or feeding organs and lack the structural hardness or delivery systems required for injecting venom."         },         {           "label": "D",           "text": "Through a stinger located on the abdomen",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "The abdomen is highly reduced and does not possess a defensive stinger like that found in some other arthropods."         }       ],       "hint": "Look for a method involving direct physical contact during the boxing-like fights between males.",       "correctAnswer": "A"     },     {       "number": 9,       "question": "The body of a skeleton shrimp is divided into several segments. Which section comprises the majority of the body's length?",       "options": [         {           "label": "A",           "text": "The pereon",           "isCorrect": true,           "rationale": "The pereon (thorax) consists of seven segments called pereonites and makes up nearly the entire visible length of these slender creatures."         },         {           "label": "B",           "text": "The urosome",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "While true for many other amphipods, the urosome and uropods are significantly diminished in the skeleton shrimp's aberrant body plan."         },         {           "label": "C",           "text": "The cephalon",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "The cephalon is the head section, which is relatively small and often fused to the first segment of the pereon."         },         {           "label": "D",           "text": "The abdomen",           "isCorrect": false,           "rationale": "In caprellids, the abdomen is highly reduced and almost invisible, located at the very end of the body."         }       ],       "hint": "This part is divided into seven segments that hold the majority of the animal's limbs.",       "correctAnswer": "A"     }   ] }

comment: The sea pig (also called a sea cucumber or Holothuroid), was one of the most common and abundant animals caught. Sea cucumbers are important in processing the sediment (like earthworms on land) but their numbers worldwide have been threatened by recent fisheries.

Gigantism (due to high oxygen levels in polar waters) was first demonstrated in amphipods and they are an important group that often take the role of animals such as crabs (which are virtually absent in Antarctica).
A scale worm, scientific name Laetmonice sp. Polychaete worms like this are often the most abundant large organisms particularly on this continental shelf (the warmest around Antarctica).
these fish are highly adapted to life in cold water with anti-freeze, but no red blood cells, in their blood.

Skeleton shrimp (Liropus minusculus). This species of skeleton shrimp was found in a reef cave off the coast of Catalina Island, California, USA. These amphipod crustaceans have translucent bodies that make them look like living skeletons, which helps them camouflage themselves against their surroundings.

Skeleton shrimp (Caprella septentrionalis). Skeleton shrimp mimic the colour and shape of the fronds of seaweed, hydroids and bryozoans amongst which they live. They use this camouflage to help ambush their prey, remaining motionless for long periods of time. Photographed in Kandalaksha Bay, White Sea, Russia.

Japanese skeleton shrimp with breeding pouch: the protrusion with the red dots. Back at home selecting and editing photo's is a time consuming job. But a rewarding one! It is a kind of arm chair marine biology. Magnifying photo's to 100% reveals a lot of creatures I hadn't recorded at the time.
Close up. The milky coloured protrusions are its gills.

Remember the film Aliens or Star Trek species 8742? Just fantasize this creature 5 meter high!  They feed by making 'kowtow's', slashing their claws - which are very big in comparison to their bodylength - forth and back. That way they collect little worms and crustaceans carried by the currents. As is quite common with crustaceans, they also cannabalise on little brothers and sisters or other species of Caprellids.

Sixteen years after his first visit to the Australian Museum, Dr José Guerra Garcia, from the Universidad de Sevilla in Spain, is back making more discoveries. These include three new species and a new genus of his favourite animals, the skeleton shrimp. These small crustaceans have been implicated as invasive in marine environments, useful bioindicators of environmental health, models for interpreting morphological evolution and a resource for aquaculture and aquarium industries, so it is important to understand their diversity, distribution and identification.  Skeleton shrimp are classified in the order of crustaceans known as the Amphipoda, which number nearly 10,000 species (80% marine, 17% freshwater and 3% terrestrial), characterised by a range of features including unique segmentation, absence of a carapace and bodies that are generally laterally compressed. Within this larger grouping the skeleton shrimp are categorised as a sub-order known as the Caprellidea (or caprellids).  There are more than 400 described species of caprellids, 50% of these are in the genus Caprella. Most are a few millimetres to centimetres in length and all are marine. The general characteristics of the Caprellidea, compared to other amphipods, include a slender and cylindrical body (the reason for the common name – skeleton shrimp), fusion of the head and the first body segment, reduction in the number of pairs of legs and leg segments, two or three pairs of gills, females with brood plates for holding eggs on only a few body segments, and a degenerated abdomen and abdominal appendages. In short, they are highly modified. This divergence from the body plan of other crustaceans makes them significant in evolutionary theory, with multiple losses or reductions of various morphological parts apparent in different lineages.

Two different species of caprellid amphipods together: on the left, Caprella acanthifera; on the right: Pthisica marina. They were on branches of the hidrozoan Obelia dichotoma, several polyps of which may be seen as well. This two amphipods didn't seem to get on well, although they look like they were neighbors...

comment: The predatory tunicate (Megalodicopia hians), also known as the ghostfish,[2] is a species of tunicate which lives anchored along deep-sea canyon walls and the seafloor. It is unique among tunicates in that, rather than being a filter feeder, it has adapted to life as an ambush predator. Its mouth-like siphon is quick to close whenever a small animal such as a crustacean or a fish drifts inside. Once the predatory tunicate catches a meal, it keeps its trap shut until the animal inside is digested. They are known to live in the Monterey Canyon at depths of 200–1,000 metres (660–3,280 ft). They mostly feed on zooplankton and other tiny animals. Their bodies are roughly 5 inches (13 cm) across.[3]

comment: This beautiful brittle star was filmed expanding its curly, branching arms out over about a minute. It was found with its arms intertwined with an octocoral. Posed here, it is able to filter feed on food floating by in the water above the seabed.

An isopod crustacean, scientific name Antarcturus sp. This group of animals (which woodlice on land belong to) are very rich in the Southern Ocean, particularly in deeper water. Antarcturus tend to hang off seaweed, sponges or (as here) sea fans to catch tiny plankton in the water.

VidComment: HMB (beta-hydroxy-beta-methylbutyrate) supplements, often taken at 3g/day, are effective at reducing muscle breakdown and increasing lean mass, particularly in untrained individuals, older adults with sarcopenia, or during intense training. Studies show it improves strength and body composition, with some evidence suggesting HMB-free acid (HMB-FA) offers faster, superior results.  Key Findings from HMB Research      Older Adults & Sarcopenia: HMB is highly effective in preventing age-related muscle loss. Research indicates 3g/day can increase limb circumference, strength, and mobility, especially when combined with vitamin     .     Athletes & Muscle Growth: While effective, benefits in trained athletes are often more subtle than in untrained individuals, acting largely to support recovery from high-intensity training.     Forms of HMB: Calcium-HMB (HMB-Ca) is the common form, but HMB-free acid (HMB-FA) appears to raise plasma levels faster.     Timing: For athletes, consuming HMB 30–60 minutes before training can maximize its acute effect on muscle damage.     Recovery: HMB has been shown to reduce muscle damage (lower creatine kinase levels) during strenuous exercise.
